description: The provided image displays a 'Check for updates' icon and does not contain directly readable scientific data. However, based on the comprehensive text extraction summary for the paper 'Deciphering the composition-microstructure correlation in low-density FeMnAlC steels with machine learning', the study investigates Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C steel processed via induction melting under Ar, homogenization at 1200°C for 2 h, forging to 60×60 mm billet, secondary homogenization at 1050°C for 100 min, hot rolling to 12 mm plate, air cooling, cold rolling with 75% thickness reduction, annealing at 1000°C for 15 min, water quenching, and aging at 550°C for 2 h. Characterization combining XRD, EBSD, TEM, EPMA, and SEM revealed a single-phase austenitic (γ) matrix with recrystallized equiaxed grains of average size 17 μm and coherent nanoscale κ-carbide precipitates. Machine learning models including Random Forest (classification accuracy 0.98) and Support Vector Machine (regression RMSE <1%) predicted κ-phase existence, (γ+κ) dual-phase regions at 500–600°C, and volume fraction. The experimental validation exhibited yield strength of 953 MPa, tensile strength of 1109 MPa, and elongation of 42%.

The figure presents a comprehensive flowchart illustrating the machine learning-based design framework for developing low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels. The workflow initiates from "Possible candidates" and progresses through a sequential "Filter" system comprising five decision criteria: existence of κ-carbide phase ("κ exists?"), presence of a single austenite (γ) region ("A single γ region?"), formation of a (γ+κ) dual-phase region within the 500-600°C temperature range, high κ phase fraction ("κ fraction high?"), and low density ("Low density?"). Each diamond-shaped decision node includes "Yes" and "No" pathways, with negative responses cycling back to the filter input. Candidates satisfying all criteria proceed to "Empirical formula" generation via "Predicting models based on machine-learning method." The process continues through "Experimental tests" to evaluate "Good properties?" with a feedback loop returning to the machine learning models if validation fails, ultimately achieving "Success" upon experimental confirmation. This framework establishes quantitative composition-microstructure correlations for accelerated alloy development.

This figure presents a Pearson correlation coefficient matrix heatmap visualizing pairwise correlations among ten input descriptors used for machine learning modeling of low-density FeMnAlC steels. The descriptors include compositional features (Mn, Al, C, Fe) and thermodynamic parameters (VEC, Tm, ΔSmix, ΔHmix, Δχ, δ). Correlation strength is encoded by circle size and color intensity, with blue indicating positive and red indicating negative correlations. Notable strong positive correlations include C with δ (0.98) and Δχ (0.97), as well as δ with Δχ (0.98), while strong negative correlations include Fe with Mn (-0.9), Al with VEC (-0.94), and C with ΔHmix (-0.93). The matrix served to eliminate redundant features prior to model training; features with correlation coefficients exceeding 0.95 (specifically Δχ and δ) were removed to prevent multicollinearity, retaining VEC, Tm, ΔSmix, ΔHmix, and compositional elements for subsequent κ-carbide phase prediction. The visualization thus establishes the feature independence necessary for robust machine learning classification and regression of microstructural outcomes.

This figure presents machine learning algorithm selection and descriptor importance analysis for phase prediction in FeMnAlC steels. Panel (a) compares cross-validation accuracy (CVA) of five algorithms—KNN, ANN, SVM, RF, and CART—for three classification tasks: Y1 (existence of κ phase), Y2 ((γ+κ) dual-phase region at 500–600°C), and Y3 (single γ region). Random Forest (RF) achieved the highest accuracy (0.98 for Y1, 0.98 for Y2, and 0.96 for Y3), while ANN performed poorly except for Y2. Panels (b)-(d) display the mean decrease accuracy (MDA) for eight compositional descriptors (Mn, Al, C, Fe, VEC, Tm, ΔSmix, ΔHmix) when removed from the dataset. For the single γ region (Y3, panel b) and κ phase existence (Y1, panel d), Al shows the highest importance (exceeding 40% and 50% decrease respectively), indicating it is the most critical element for these phase stabilities. For the (γ+κ) region at 500–600°C (Y2, panel c), Mn and Fe exhibit the highest importance (approximately 15% decrease), while C consistently shows the lowest impact across all three classifications.

This figure presents a comprehensive evaluation of machine learning models for predicting high κ-phase fraction in low-density FeMnAlC steels. Panel (a) compares five regression algorithms—KNN, ANN, SVM, RF, and CART—based on 10-fold cross-validation RMSE values, where SVM demonstrates superior performance with RMSE below 1%, while CART exhibits the highest error exceeding 2%. Panel (b) illustrates the relative importance of eight descriptors (Mn, Al, C, Fe, VEC, Tm, ΔSmix, and ΔHmix) through a feature removal analysis, showing that removing Al causes the most significant increase in RMSE, followed by VEC, indicating their critical roles in predicting κ-phase fraction. Panel (c) validates the SVM model's predictive capability through a parity plot comparing predicted versus true κ-phase fraction values, achieving a high correlation coefficient of R=0.99. Collectively, these results establish SVM as the optimal algorithm for this regression task and identify the key compositional and thermodynamic parameters governing κ-phase formation, supporting the data-driven design of FeMnAlC steels with targeted microstructures.

This figure presents a two-step machine learning-based screening workflow for optimizing Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel compositions. The schematic illustrates a progressive reduction of the design space from an initial pool of 78,477 candidates (Area A) to 1,320 candidates (Area B) and finally to 150 optimized candidates (Area C). The first screening step applies boundary conditions requiring κ-phase existence, a single γ-austenite region, and a (γ+κ) dual-phase region within 500–600°C, reducing candidates to 1,320. The second step further filters based on κ-phase fraction ≥10% and density ≤7 g/cm³, yielding 150 final candidates. The visual metaphor of decreasing dot density in circular fields effectively represents the narrowing search space. This screening strategy supports the paper's core conclusion that machine learning can efficiently identify compositions simultaneously satisfying phase stability (κ-carbide precipitation), low density, and mechanical property requirements for advanced lightweight steels.

This figure presents a five-panel composition-structure optimization map for FeMnAlC austenitic low-density steels with fixed 30 wt.% Mn, displaying machine learning screening results in the Al-C compositional space (Al: 2–12 wt.%, C: 0–2.2 wt.%). Panel (a) marks the existence region of the κ phase (Y1) in red, indicating that κ-carbide precipitates when Al content exceeds approximately 5 wt.% across the investigated C range. Panel (b) identifies the γ+κ dual-phase region (Y2) at 500–600°C, confined to intermediate Al (approximately 4.5–9.3 wt.%) and C (0.4–1.1 wt.%) contents. Panel (c) delineates the single γ-phase region (Y3) in red, showing the austenite stability field with an upper Al limit near 11.6 wt.%. Panel (d) illustrates the intersection of Y1 and Y3 (red zone), representing compositions where κ phase exists within a single-phase austenite matrix at equilibrium. Panel (e) shows the triple intersection of Y1, Y2, and Y3 (red zone), pinpointing optimal compositions satisfying all three constraints: κ phase existence, single γ-phase matrix, and γ+κ duplex stability at 500–600°C. The maps utilize blue dots for excluded compositions and red/black dots for selected regions, visually demonstrating how Al governs κ phase precipitation while C modulates the dual-phase temperature window.

This figure presents the second-step machine learning-based performance screening for low-density FeMnAlC steels, illustrating composition optimization via contour maps and scatter plots. Panel (a) displays a contour map of predicted κ-carbide volume fraction (Y₄) as functions of aluminum (5.0–9.0 wt.%) and carbon (0.4–1.1 wt.%) content, with color intensity indicating volume fractions ranging from 0.45% to 13.15%. Panel (c) shows the corresponding density contour map (Υ₅), revealing values decreasing from 7.234 to 6.812 g/cm³ with increasing aluminum content. Panel (b) delineates the screening protocol where blue circles represent compositions meeting the κ fraction target (≥11.000%) and green circles represent density targets (≤6.900 g/cm³), with overlapping regions identifying 30 viable candidates satisfying dual constraints; the red star marks the experimentally validated composition Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C. This demonstrates the ML-guided optimization workflow for identifying lightweight, high-strength steel compositions.

Figure 8 presents comprehensive multi-scale microstructural characterization of Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel (measured composition: Mn 30.4 wt%, Al 7.9 wt%, C 1.1 wt%) after aging treatment. Panel (a) shows the XRD profile revealing single-phase FCC austenite (γ) with characteristic diffraction peaks at (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) positions. Panels (b) and (c) display EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) and phase maps respectively, confirming a fully recrystallized microstructure with equiaxed grains averaging 17 μm and exclusively FCC phase (red). Panels (d) and (e) present bright-field and dark-field TEM micrographs highlighting nanoscale κ-carbide precipitates distributed within the γ matrix. Panel (f) shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern indexed along the [011̄] zone axis, verifying the FCC structure and κ phase presence. These results collectively validate the predicted (γ + κ) dual-phase structure, where high-density nanoscale κ carbides provide precipitation strengthening.

Figure 9 presents comprehensive experimental validation of the machine learning-designed Fe-30Mn-8Al-1C (wt%) low-density steel following annealing at 1000°C for 15 min, water quenching, and aging at 550°C for 2 h. Panel (a) displays electron probe microanalysis (EPMA) element distribution maps for Fe, Mn, Al, and C alongside secondary electron (SEI) and backscattered electron (COMPO) images (scale bar 20 μm), confirming uniform elemental distribution without macroscopic segregation. Panel (b) presents the engineering stress-strain curve, revealing exceptional mechanical properties with a yield strength of 953 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 1109 MPa, and elongation of 42%. Panel (c) shows an optical micrograph (scale bar 50 μm) of the recrystallized equiaxed austenitic grain structure with an average grain size of approximately 17 μm. These results collectively validate the predicted composition-microstructure correlation, confirming the alloy's single-phase austenitic matrix with nanoscale κ-carbide precipitates and its superior strength-ductility synergy.
